candidate_problems.text: What precise structural threshold turns ordinary positive feedback into recursive control over the market’s evaluation function? The conjecture identifies a loop from success to visibility to adoption to rule-setting, but leaves open a discriminating criterion for when influence over outcomes becomes influence over selection itself. A worthwhile problem is to formulate measurable indicators or a dynamical model that distinguishes strong but still contestable network effects from true self-referential closure.

How can one separate efficient standardization from pathological winner-take-all recursion? The conjecture treats becoming 'the standard' as a source of vulnerability, but many standards are socially useful and reduce coordination costs. The unresolved tension is identifying when dominance reflects genuine problem-solving versus when it mainly persists because incumbents shape recognition, trust, and rules in their own favor. This is crucial if the conjecture is to guide intervention without attacking beneficial scale or common standards.

Which interventions actually break the strange loop without degrading the underlying system’s capacity to coordinate, learn, and innovate? The conjecture proposes interoperability, portability, anti-self-preferencing, and decentralized discovery, but does not explain the conditions under which these alter the feedback structure rather than merely shifting power to new gatekeepers or reducing useful feedback. The open problem is to map the causal pathways and tradeoffs of loop-interrupting policies across different market types.
